speaker: interviewee
text: I told Sarah all about you. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to do the star first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'll do the star first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Just lean it against the-- yeah, I've got a very tight shot. OK. Nobody sees. Yeah, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, now, it has to be straight, straight, straight.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yeah, you could just hold it from the top. I think that's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This Jewish star was given to me by a young man that I met after we were liberated. They had-- they had been sent from Holland to Bergen-Belsen at the end of the war. They were going to be exchanged for money through the Swiss consulate or something like that. These were Jewish people who wore stars, and they still wore their, their regular clothing. And I was so shocked when I saw them because I didn't think any Jews were alive at that time, much less Jews who were still wearing normal clothes. And I was so overtaken with it that I talked to him. And he told me that he has the star, and that he gave it to me as a memento. So this is an original star that was worn by Jews in Holland, [BACKGROUND NOISE] in the ghettos during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a 5 kronen note, which was actually the legal tender of the Lodz ghetto. And this was given to me by someone who had actually survived the Lodz ghetto. And he had some of this money along with some of the pictures that he managed to hold onto, throughout the whole ordeal. He was a friend. I don't even remember his name. But I knew, even at the time, that this was going to be the memento one day that's going to be, perhaps, needed and valuable. So I'm holding on to these two things, which I intend to have my children inherit one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now-- can we take this away now?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Keep it right there for a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say it's the other side. Just tell them it's the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is the other side of the 5, 5 kronen, or whatever they're called, note, which depicts Moses with the Ten Commandments. I guess it was done by Jewish people and designed by Jewish people. It was legal tender. You could probably buy for the five kronen I don't know what, but very little, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a note given to me by a young woman named Flora Levine, who was the head of the office of the American Joint Distribution Committee in Stuttgart, where I took a job with her in 1945 or '46-- the end of '45, beginning of '46-- for several months. I worked to help out in her office. It is, in fact, Flora Levine who signed an affidavit for my trip to the United States. You had to have a sponsor, and she signed for me. And this is how I came to the United States. I had absolutely no relatives here. If you can-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a letter of recommendation that Flora Levine gave me when I left for the United States. And believe it or not, I've used it on many occasions. In fact, this was like a passport for any job really. I remember once applying for a job [BACKGROUND NOISE] where there are about 20 other young men vying for this job. And I didn't think I had much of a chance, because the requirements were so great. And these boys were all high school graduates, you know. That was a requirement. I was not high school graduate, unfortunately. But I decided to stay and take my chances. And when my turn came, I whipped out this letter of recommendation, and I showed it to the boss. And he looked at it, and he read it. And he looked at me. He read it again. And, you know, he said he was going to let me know. When I walked out of that office, I knew I had the job. And I really did. He called that evening, and he told me that I won out over 20 other young people at the time. It was unbelievable. The job didn't turn out to be my future. But it was very uplifting to-- for that to have happened to me. So I consider this to be one of the important mementos that I keep.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when I-- [LAUGHS] maybe you ought to leave the room. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I finished my story, when I read about what happened after the liberation, and I walked into the ballroom, and I heard the orchestra play. And this girl that came over to me, her name was Renia, that was the girl. That was Renia. And this is where-- this is where-- I danced with her and where my memoirs, my book, ends. And I would like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just a memento of the picture that she gave me. We had a very short, intense friendship. And as she-- then we just parted. But it is a very dear memento for me. Because it was the first encounter with a girl that awakened me to the fact that I was a human being like everybody else. And it started all these feelings that we take for granted. It restarted them in me. It was probably maybe two weeks after I was liberated or so that I met her. And for that reason, I treasure this very much. Besides, she was a very nice girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This picture was taken in Stuttgart, which was a DP camp. And the time, probably was at the end of 19-- or sometime in 1946, right? Sometime in 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, my wife, Danka, is holding a basketball. And I'm holding on to her, for some reason. I don't know why. But that was probably the beginning of this beautiful friendship that eventually brought us together. I remember the time when this was taken very well. We were together in Stuttgart, and we were friends in Stuttgart. And then I left at the end of 1946 for America. And my wife Dinah did not follow me for another two years. She came here in July 1949. And several months later on November '49, we were married. But this was the beginning of the beautiful friendship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep it right there. I'm going to just try and get closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself, taken probably several weeks after we were liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For better, for worse, this is what I looked like. I had already gained a few pounds, but I was still pretty skinny. Probably no more than 120 pounds at the time. But a full head of hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my uncle, Mordechai. He was my mother's younger brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We, we, we didn't know him that well. Because he lived in Warsaw, we lived in Radom. And he would come at least two, three times a year, to visit with us. I remember him fondly. He was very well-to-do. And whenever he came, he left me some money. And so I found his picture because he had send it to a distant relative that they had, many, many years ago. And I asked him for it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I actually remember him the way he looks here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, on my left side, which is your right side, is my brother, Eli. And this was taken probably in either 1937 or 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other person I know very well. In fact, the other person survived. And he is still alive and well to this day. They were just friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His name is Borenstein. I don't recall his first name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ziska Borenstein. And he is-- he is alive, he's well. He's the one who gave me the picture. For some reason, he was able to safeguard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in Pionki, either in 1937 or 1938. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In, [POINTS] in the back, where I point, is my mother. That is the best picture that I have of her. I have no other. She-- and in front, [POINTS] right here where I point, is my sister, Bluma. The other people are relatives who happened to be-- to come out to visit with us. There's a [POINTING] young child, who didn't survive. There's this young man, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], who did not survive either. There is this woman in the back, [POINTING] who is the mother of her.  She is a relative. And her name was Sarah. And she did not survive. The only person in this picture that survived is this woman here, who is the mother of the child. When-- when she came to Auschwitz, she-- she had to give up the child. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is the only thing that I have of my sister and my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is an actual prewar picture. It's the only one that I have of part of my family. It was taken at a wedding reception, just before the war. And I don't remember how I got this. But someone gave it to me. [POINTING] It's probably one of the two sisters that survived. In this picture, there's my brother, Eli, right here, this, this head. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This, with the white collar, is my sister, Bluma. And this little head sticking out between them is myself, if you can make anything out of that. So the three of us are in this picture. And I was then 12 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture taken of myself and, and my wife, Dinah, probably a few days after she came to the United States. As I said, I came here in the beginning of 1947. She came in the summer of '49. So this is the summer of '49. And if I remember correctly, it's probably one of the first pictures we took, the two of us together, after her arrival. OK?
